cards.text: A smart pointer is a {{c1::composition}} class that manages dynamically allocated memory and ensures it gets {{c2::deleted}} when the smart pointer goes {{c3::out of scope}}.

cards.front: What are the three main smart pointer types in C++11 and their ownership models?
cards.back:
• std::unique_ptr - exclusive ownership
  • std::shared_ptr - shared ownership (reference counting)
  • std::weak_ptr - non-owning observer

cards.text: Move semantics allows us to {{c1::transfer ownership}} of resources from one object to another, instead of making {{c2::expensive copies}}.

cards.question: Which smart pointer should you use for each scenario?
cards.answer:
1: std::unique_ptr (exclusive ownership)
2: std::shared_ptr (shared ownership)
3: std::weak_ptr (non-owning observer)
cards.explanation: Default to unique_ptr. Use shared_ptr for shared ownership. Use weak_ptr to break circular references.

cards.front: What's the key difference between l-values and r-values?
cards.back:
L-values have persistent identity and addressable memory (e.g., variables).
R-values are temporary or about to be destroyed (e.g., literals, temporaries, moved-from objects).

cards.text: Move operations should be marked {{c1::noexcept}} to enable optimizations in STL containers. After moving, leave the source in a {{c2::valid but unspecified}} state.

cards.explanation:
Decision tree for choosing smart pointers:
  • Single ownership? → std::unique_ptr
  • Multiple owners? → std::shared_ptr
  • Observer only (no ownership)? → std::weak_ptr
  • Breaking circular ref? → std::weak_ptr
  • Not sure? → Start with std::unique_ptr

cards.question: When should you NOT use std::move?
cards.explanation: 1: Prevents RVO. 2: const prevents move, will copy instead. 3: Correct use - transferring ownership.

cards.front: Why should you prefer std::make_unique and std::make_shared over using new directly?
cards.back:
Benefits:
  • Exception-safe
  • More efficient (make_shared does single allocation)
  • More concise
  • Impossible to accidentally leak with make_*

cards.text: The Rule of Five states: if you define {{c1::destructor}}, {{c2::copy constructor}}, {{c3::copy assignment}}, {{c4::move constructor}}, or {{c5::move assignment}}, you should probably define all five.

cards.explanation:
Circular references with shared_ptr cause memory leaks. Solutions:
  • Use weak_ptr for back-references
  • Use weak_ptr for observers
  • Parent owns child (shared/unique_ptr), child observes parent (weak_ptr)
  • Redesign to avoid cycles

cards.front: What are the key takeaways from Chapter 22?
cards.back:
• Always prefer smart pointers over raw owning pointers
  • Default to std::unique_ptr
  • Use std::move to enable move semantics on l-values
  • Mark move operations noexcept
  • Use std::weak_ptr to break circular references
  • Prefer std::make_unique and std::make_shared
